findings.title: Database credentials stored in plaintext ConfigMap
findings.description: Task 1 (subtask 1004) stores DATABASE_URL and REDIS_URL — including passwords — in a Kubernetes ConfigMap (`notifycore-infra-endpoints`). ConfigMaps are not encrypted, are visible in the Kubernetes API to anyone with namespace read access, and are often logged or exposed in pod describe output. This is a significant credential leak risk.
findings.recommendation: Store connection passwords exclusively in Kubernetes Secrets. Use separate environment variables: ConfigMap for host/port/database name, Secret for credentials. The application should compose the full connection URL at startup, or mount both resources via `envFrom`. Update the Deployment manifest to reference both ConfigMapRef and SecretRef.

findings.title: No database connection encryption (TLS) configured
findings.description: The CloudNativePG Cluster CR (subtask 1002) and the DATABASE_URL configuration do not specify TLS/SSL for PostgreSQL connections. The Rust service (task 2) uses `tls-rustls` in sqlx dependencies but there is no corresponding server-side certificate configuration or `sslmode=require` in the connection string. Traffic between the app and database traverses the cluster network unencrypted.
findings.recommendation: Configure CloudNativePG with `spec.postgresql.pg_hba` entries enforcing `hostssl` and enable server certificates. Add `sslmode=require` (or `verify-full`) to DATABASE_URL. For production, configure mutual TLS using CloudNativePG's built-in certificate management.

findings.title: No authentication or authorization on API endpoints
findings.description: All five REST endpoints (POST, GET, GET list, DELETE, GET /health) are publicly accessible with no authentication mechanism. There is no mention of API keys, JWT tokens, OAuth2, or any RBAC/permissions layer. Any network-accessible client can create, read, list, and cancel notifications.
findings.recommendation: Add an authentication middleware layer (e.g., API key validation via header, JWT bearer token verification, or mTLS). At minimum, require an `Authorization` or `X-API-Key` header on all `/api/v1/*` endpoints. The `/health` endpoint can remain unauthenticated but consider separating liveness (unauthenticated) from readiness (authenticated) probes. Ingress-level authentication (e.g., OAuth2 proxy) can supplement but should not replace application-level auth.

findings.title: Insufficient input validation on notification creation
findings.description: The POST /api/v1/notifications endpoint (subtask 2004) only validates that title and body are non-empty. There are no length limits on title, body, or other string fields. An attacker could submit extremely large payloads (multi-MB title/body), causing excessive database storage consumption, memory pressure, and potential DoS.
findings.recommendation: Add maximum length validation: title (e.g., max 500 chars), body (e.g., max 10,000 chars). Validate that `channel` and `priority` are valid enum variants (serde may handle this but confirm error messages don't leak internals). Add a request body size limit via tower-http's `RequestBodyLimitLayer` (e.g., 1MB). Add VARCHAR length constraints in the database migration as a defense-in-depth measure.

findings.title: No rate limiting at the application level
findings.description: While the Ingress (task 3003) adds nginx rate limiting at 100 RPS, there is no application-level rate limiting. In development or if the ingress is bypassed (internal service-to-service calls), the API is vulnerable to abuse. Additionally, the ingress rate limit is per-IP, which may not protect against distributed attacks.
findings.recommendation: Add application-level rate limiting using tower middleware (e.g., `tower::limit::RateLimitLayer` or a custom middleware using Redis as a rate limit store). Implement per-client rate limits if API keys are added. Consider different rate limits for different endpoints (write endpoints more restrictive than reads).

findings.title: Dependency versions not fully pinned
findings.description: Cargo.toml dependencies (subtask 2001) use major version specifiers (e.g., `axum 0.7`, `tokio 1`, `redis 0.25`). While Cargo.lock will pin exact versions, the Bitnami Redis Helm subchart uses `~18.x` (subtask 1003), which allows minor version updates that could introduce vulnerabilities or breaking changes. No dependency auditing step is defined beyond clippy.
findings.recommendation: Pin Helm subchart to an exact version (e.g., `18.19.4` instead of `~18.x`). Add `cargo audit` to the CI pipeline to check for known vulnerabilities in Rust dependencies. Add a scheduled CI job for dependency updates (e.g., Dependabot or Renovate). Ensure Cargo.lock is committed to the repository.

findings.title: Container image not scanned for vulnerabilities
findings.description: The Dockerfile (subtask 2009) uses `rust:1.75-slim` and `debian:bookworm-slim` base images, and the CI/CD pipeline (subtask 3008) builds and pushes Docker images without any vulnerability scanning step. Known CVEs in base images or installed packages would go undetected.
findings.recommendation: Add a container image scanning step in the CD pipeline after the Docker build (e.g., Trivy, Grype, or Snyk container scan). Fail the pipeline on critical/high severity CVEs. Consider using `gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12` as the runtime base image instead of `debian:bookworm-slim` to minimize attack surface. Pin the base image digest rather than just the tag.

findings.title: No Pod Security Standards enforced
findings.description: There is no mention of Pod Security Admission (PSA), PodSecurityPolicies, or securityContext settings on any of the pod/container specs. The notifycore container could potentially run as root, have writable root filesystem, or gain unnecessary Linux capabilities.
findings.recommendation: Add securityContext to the Deployment template: `runAsNonRoot: true`, `runAsUser: 65534`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`, `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, drop all capabilities (`capabilities: {drop: [ALL]}`). Apply the `restricted` Pod Security Standard to the notifycore namespace via labels (`pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted`). Ensure the Dockerfile creates a non-root user.

findings.title: No security event logging or audit trail
findings.description: While structured logging is configured (JSON tracing), there is no specific security event logging — no logging of authentication failures (because auth doesn't exist yet), no audit trail for notification creation/deletion, no anomalous behavior detection. The DELETE endpoint changes notification state but the action is not logged with actor information.
findings.recommendation: Log all state-changing operations (POST, DELETE) with request metadata (client IP, user agent, and authenticated identity once auth is added). Add audit-level log entries for sensitive operations. Include correlation IDs (request ID) in all log entries via tower-http middleware. In production, forward logs to a centralized system (ELK, Loki, Datadog) and create alerts for anomalous patterns (e.g., spike in 4xx errors, unusual deletion rates).

findings.title: Redis caching may expose sensitive data without encryption
findings.description: The Redis caching layer (subtask 2006) stores serialized notification objects with a 5-minute TTL. Redis data is unencrypted at rest and in transit (no TLS configured for Redis connections). If notifications contain sensitive content, this data is exposed.
findings.recommendation: Enable Redis TLS by configuring `tls.enabled: true` in the Bitnami Redis Helm values and updating REDIS_URL to use `rediss://` scheme. Evaluate whether notification content is sensitive enough to warrant encryption at the application level before caching. Consider whether cached data needs to be sanitized.

findings.title: Helm test connectivity job may leak credentials in logs
findings.description: The Helm test Job (subtask 1005) runs `psql $DATABASE_URL` and `redis-cli -u $REDIS_URL PING`, which could log the full connection URLs including passwords in pod logs or Kubernetes events.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables and avoid passing credentials as command-line arguments (which appear in `/proc/cmdline` and pod specs). Use `psql` with `PGPASSWORD` environment variable or `.pgpass` file instead of embedding the password in the URL. For redis-cli, use `REDISCLI_AUTH` environment variable.

findings.title: Dockerfile uses a potentially outdated Rust base image
findings.description: The Dockerfile pins `rust:1.75-slim` which may become outdated. Rust 1.75 was released in December 2023 and may have known toolchain issues. There is no process to update the base image version.
findings.recommendation: Use the latest stable Rust version or parameterize it as a build argument. Add a Dependabot or Renovate configuration to track Dockerfile base image updates. Consider using `rust:1.XX-slim` where XX is managed by CI automation.

findings.title: No CORS origin restriction configured
findings.description: The Axum service adds CorsLayer (subtask 2001) but no specific allowed origins are configured. The default `tower-http` CorsLayer is permissive, allowing any origin. This could enable cross-origin attacks if the API is browser-accessible.
findings.recommendation: Configure CorsLayer with explicit allowed origins matching your frontend domain(s). For API-only services, consider restricting to specific origins or disabling CORS if the API is not intended to be called from browsers. At minimum, use `CorsLayer::new().allow_origin(...)` with a whitelist parameterized from environment variables.

findings.title: No encryption at rest for PostgreSQL data
findings.description: The CloudNativePG configuration does not specify encryption at rest for the persistent volumes. Notification data (which could contain PII depending on use case) would be stored unencrypted on disk. Backup data (subtask 3001) going to object storage also has no encryption configuration mentioned.
findings.recommendation: Enable encryption at rest via the Kubernetes StorageClass (e.g., AWS EBS encryption, GCE PD encryption, or dm-crypt). For backups to object storage, enable server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS) on the backup bucket. Configure CloudNativePG's `spec.backup.barmanObjectStore.encryption` if supported. Document the encryption requirements in the infrastructure runbook.

strengths: Network Policies are comprehensive with default-deny and explicit allow rules, covering app-to-database, app-to-Redis, ingress-to-app, and DNS egress — this is a strong microsegmentation approach.
Graceful shutdown is planned from the start with `tokio::signal::ctrl_c()` and Axum's `with_graceful_shutdown`, preventing data loss during pod termination.
PodDisruptionBudgets are configured for PostgreSQL, Redis, and the application Deployment, ensuring availability during cluster maintenance.
ServiceAccount with `automountServiceAccountToken: false` minimizes the blast radius of a container compromise by preventing access to the Kubernetes API.
ResourceQuota and HPA are properly planned for production, preventing resource exhaustion and enabling automatic scaling under load.
Structured JSON logging with environment-configurable log levels via `RUST_LOG` enables good observability foundations.
CI/CD pipeline includes `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` and `cargo sqlx prepare --check`, catching code quality issues and query mismatches early.
summary: The task plan has strong infrastructure foundations with network policies, PDBs, RBAC, and scaling configurations, but has critical gaps in authentication (no API auth at all), credential management (passwords in ConfigMaps), and data protection (no TLS for database connections, no encryption at rest). The most urgent issues to address before production deployment are: adding API authentication, moving credentials out of ConfigMaps into Secrets, enforcing TLS on all database/cache connections, and adding input validation length limits. Without these, the service would be vulnerable to unauthorized access, credential exposure, and denial-of-service attacks.
